params.text:
Working in a person-centred way

Person-centred care means shaping the support you give around the individual — their needs, wishes, preferences and their whole life story — rather than fitting the person into a fixed routine. It treats the person as the expert in their own life and as an equal partner in decisions about their care, not a passive recipient of tasks.

This is the heart of Care Certificate Standard 5, "Work in a person-centred way", and it runs through the guidance of the Care Quality Commission (CQC), the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) and the Social Care Institute for Excellence (SCIE).

  • It is the opposite of a "one size fits all" approach.
  • It focuses on the outcomes that matter to the person, not simply on completing jobs.
  • It is an ongoing process — what matters to someone can change from day to day.

Why it matters

When care is genuinely person-centred, people feel known, valued and in control of their own lives. This is not a "nice to have" — it directly affects wellbeing and safety.

  • It protects dignity and builds self-worth and identity.
  • It builds trust, so people are more willing to engage with their care and share concerns.
  • It leads to better outcomes and a better quality of life.
  • It helps prevent abuse and neglect, because the person’s voice stays at the centre.

Under CQC Regulation 9, providers must give care and treatment that is appropriate, meets people’s needs and reflects their personal preferences.
